B. The producer does not wait for a response from the consumer.

C. The producer goes to sleep.

D. The producer waits for a timeout.
cards.definition: B. The producer does not wait for a response from the consumer.

cards.newWord:
A producer sends a message to a consumer. Which one of the following is synchronous message communication with reply?

A. The producer waits for a response from the consumer.

B. The producer does not wait for a response from the consumer.

C. The producer goes to sleep.

D. The producer waits for a timeout.
cards.definition: A. The producer waits for a response from the consumer.

cards.newWord:
What is an integrated communication diagram?

B. A synthesis of all the communication diagrams developed to support the use cases

C. A communication diagram depicting the objects that realize a use case

cards.definition: B. A synthesis of all the communication diagrams developed to support the use cases

cards.newWord:
____ is an integrated communication diagram?

A. A communication diagram formed by combining objects

B. A communication diagram synthesis of all relevant use case based communication diagrams showing all objects and their interactions

C. A communication diagram synthesis of all the objects that realize a use case

cards.definition: B. A communication diagram synthesis of all relevant use case based communication diagrams showing all objects and their interactions

cards.newWord:
Which of the following objects should be assigned to the same subsystem?

A. Objects that are part of the same composite object

B. Client and server objects

C. User interface and entity objects

D. Objects that are associated with each other
cards.definition: A. Objects that are part of the same composite object

cards.newWord:
Objects that are in geographically different locations should be:

A. In the same subsystem

B. In different subsystems

C. In a composite subsystem

D. In layered subsystems
cards.definition: B. In different subsystems

cards.newWord:
If scope of control is used in subsystem structuring, then:

B. A state-dependent control object is placed in the same subsystem as the objects it controls.

C. A state-dependent control object is placed in a different subsystem from the objects it controls.

cards.definition: B. A state-dependent control object is placed in the same subsystem as the objects it controls.

cards.newWord:
How should an external object be designed to interface to the system?

A. It should interface to one subsystem.

B. It should interface to several subsystems.

C. It should interface to every subsystem.

D. It should interface to none of the subsystems.
cards.definition: A. It should interface to one subsystem.

cards.newWord:
A user interface subsystem is a type of:

A. Control subsystem

B. Service subsystem

C. Client subsystem

D. I/O subsystem
cards.definition: C. Client subsystem

cards.newWord:
Which of the following objects are NOT likely to be in the same subsystem?

A. User interface object and entity object

B. State-dependent control object and coordinator object

C. Business logic object and entity object

D. I/O object and state-dependent control object
cards.definition: B. State-dependent control object and coordinator object

cards.newWord:
Which of the following subsystems is NOT likely to be a client subsystem?

A. Control subsystem

B. User interaction subsystem

C. Service subsystem

D. I/O subsystem
cards.definition: C. Service subsystem

cards.newWord:
When is a coordinator subsystem required?

A. If the subsystem needs to coordinate several internal objects

B. If the subsystem needs to coordinate multiple I/O devices

C. If the subsystem receives messages from multiple client subsystems

D. If the subsystem needs to coordinate the execution of other subsystems
cards.definition: D. If the subsystem needs to coordinate the execution of other subsystems

cards.newWord:
When is a control subsystem required?

A. If the subsystem needs to control several internal objects

B. If the subsystem needs to control multiple I/O devices

C. If the subsystem needs to control multiple client subsystems

D. If the subsystem needs to control the execution of other subsystems
cards.definition: A. If the subsystem needs to control several internal objects

cards.newWord:
What is an information hiding object?

A. An active object that encapsulates data

B. A passive object that encapsulates data

C. A class that encapsulates data

D. A task that encapsulates data
cards.definition: B. A passive object that encapsulates data

cards.newWord:
What is a class interface?

A. Specifies the internals of the operations of a class

B. Specifies the externally visible operations of a class

C. Specifies the parameters of a class operation.

D. Specifies the signature of a class operation
cards.definition: B. Specifies the externally visible operations of a class

cards.newWord:
____ specifies the externally visible operations of a class, service, or composer internal structure of the operations

A. An interface

B. A class

C. An object

D. A service
cards.definition: A. An interface

cards.newWord:
Which of the following is NOT an object-oriented concept?

A. Information hiding

B. Class

C. Subclass

D. Subroutine
cards.definition: D. Subroutine

cards.newWord:
Which of the following is a class that realizes an interface?

A. The class calls the interface.

B. The class implements the interface.

C. The class is called by the interface.

D. The class is independent of the interface
cards.definition: C. The class is called by the interface.

cards.newWord:
Which of the following is an entity class?

A. An information hiding class

B. A subclass

C. A control class

D. A data abstraction class
cards.definition: D. A data abstraction class

cards.newWord:
What does a state machine class encapsulate?

A. A state transition table

B. A statechart

C. The current state of the machine

D. A state transition table and the current state of the machine
cards.definition: D. A state transition table and the current state of the machine

cards.newWord:
Which of the following is unlikely to be a graphical user interface class?

A. A menu

B. A window

C. A button

D. A pin
cards.definition: D. A pin

cards.newWord:
Which of the following is unlikely to be encapsulated in a business logic class?

A. A business rule

B. Calls to operations of an entity class

D. A dialog box
cards.definition: D. A dialog box

cards.newWord:
Which of the following is NOT allowed through inheritance?

A. Subclass inherits attributes from superclass.

B. Subclass inherits operations from superclass.

D. Subclass redefines operations inherited from superclass.
cards.definition: C. Subclass redefines attributes inherited from superclass.

cards.newWord:
Which of the following is true for an abstract class?

A. It is used as a template for creating objects.

B. It is used as a template for creating subclasses.

C. It is used as a template for creating classes.

D. It is used as a template for creating superclasses.
cards.definition: B. It is used as a template for creating subclasses.

cards.newWord:
In object-oriented design, polymorphism means that:

A. Different classes may have the same name.

C. Different classes may have the same superclass name.

D. Different classes may have the same operation name
cards.definition: D. Different classes may have the same operation name

cards.newWord:
With polymorphism and dynamic binding, an object can:

A. Invoke operation of different names on the same objects.

B. Invoke operations of different names on different objects.

C. Invoke an operation of the same name on the same object.

D. Invoke an operation of the same name on different objects.
cards.definition: D. Invoke an operation of the same name on different objects.

cards.newWord:
What is a server?

A. A hardware/software system that serves customers

B. A subsystem that makes requests and waits for the responses

cards.definition: D. A hardware/software system that provides one or more services for multiple clients

cards.newWord:
The basic client/single service architectural pattern states that:

A. Multiple clients request services, and multiple services fulfill client requests.

B. Multiple clients request services, and a service fulfills client requests.

C. A client requests services, and a service fulfills client requests.

cards.definition: B. Multiple clients request services, and a service fulfills client requests.

cards.newWord:
In a Multi-tier Client/Service architectural pattern, which of the following is

true about an intermediate tier?

A. An intermediate tier is a client tier.

B. An intermediate tier is a service tier.

D. An intermediate tier is both a client tier and a service tier.
cards.definition: D. An intermediate tier is both a client tier and a service tier.

cards.newWord:
How is Multiple Client/Multiple Service architectural pattern different from a

Multiple Client/Single Service architectural pattern?

A. A service can receive requests from multiple clients.

B. A client can send requests to multiple services.

C. A client can send requests to other clients.

D. A service can respond to requests from multiple clients.
cards.definition: B. A client can send requests to multiple services.

cards.newWord:
How is a sequential service designed?

A. One object that responds to requests from clients

B. Multiple objects that respond to requests from clients

C. One subsystem that responds to requests from clients

D. Multiple subsystems that respond to requests from clients
cards.definition: A. One object that responds to requests from clients

cards.newWord:
How is a concurrent service designed?

A. One object that responds to requests from clients

B. Multiple objects that respond to requests from clients

D. Multiple subsystems that respond to requests from clients
cards.definition: B. Multiple objects that respond to requests from clients

cards.newWord:
What is a database wrapper class?

A. A class that encapsulates a data structure

B. A class that encapsulates a database

cards.definition: C. A class that encapsulates the details of how to access data in a database

cards.newWord:
When designing an entity class as a relational table, which of the following is

NOT true?

A. The relational table has multiple primary keys.

cards.definition: A. The relational table has multiple primary keys.

following is NOT true?

cards.definition: C. The aggregate and part classes are designed as relational tables.

cards.newWord:
What is a service-oriented architecture (SOA)?

B. A distributed software architecture consisting of multiple autonomous services

cards.definition: B. A distributed software architecture consisting of multiple autonomous services

cards.newWord:
Which of the following properties DOES NOT apply to a service?

B. Discoverable

C. Fixed

D. Autonomous
cards.definition: C. Fixed

In a SOA, which of the following is NOT true?

cards.definition: A. A client communicates with a specific service provided on a fixed server configuration.

cards.newWord:

cards.definition: D. An object that mediates interactions between clients and services

B. So that a service can interrogate the broker

cards.definition: B. Knows the type of service required but not the specific service

What does a component's required interface consist of?

cards.definition: D. An active object has a thread of control

cards.definition: C. A task that receives inputs from an external device when it generates interrupts

What is a periodic task?

cards.definition: B. A task that is activated by a timer event

What is a demand-driven task?

What is a software product line (SPL)?

cards.definition: A. A family of systems with some common components and some variable components

What is an optional use case?

cards.definition:
B. A member of the SPL composed of kernel classes and possibly some

default classes

cards.definition: B. Software nonfunctional requirements

What is maintainability?

cards.definition: A. Can be traced back to products of previous phases

What is an actor in a use case?
A. An object inside the system
B. A person who performs on stage
C. An external entity that interacts with the system
D. The customer to whom the system will be delivered
cards.definition: C. An external entity that interacts with the system

cards.newWord:
What is a primary actor?
A. The actor who goes on stage first
B. The actor that starts the use case
C. An actor that participates in the use case
D. An object inside the system
cards.definition: B. The actor that starts the use case

cards.newWord:
What is a secondary actor?
A. The actor who goes on stage second
B. The actor that starts the use case
C. An actor that participates in the use case
D. An object inside the system
cards.definition: C. An actor that participates in the use case

cards.newWord:
What is an alternative sequence in a use case?
A. A sequence that describes an error case
B. A sequence that is different from the main sequence
C. A sequence that describes interactions with a secondary actor
D. A sequence that describes interactions with a primary actor
cards.definition: B. A sequence that is different from the main sequence

cards.newWord:
What can an inclusion use case be used for?
A. To describe an inclusive use case
B. To describe a lengthy interaction with an actor
C. To describe functionality that is common to more than one use case
D. To describe a use case that includes other use cases
cards.definition: C. To describe functionality that is common to more than one use case

cards.newWord:
What can an inclusion use case be used for?
A. To describe common sequences of interactions in several use cases, which can then be extracted reused
B. To describe a use case that calls other use cases
C. To describe functionality that other use cases can use
D. To describe a use case that includes other use cases
cards.definition: C. To describe functionality that other use cases can use

cards.newWord:
What can an extension use case be used for?
A. To describe a lengthy interaction with an actor
B. To describe functionality that is common to more than one use case
C. To describe the functionality of a use case that is extended by another use case(s)
D. To describe a conditional part of a different use case that is only executed under certain circumstances
cards.definition: D. To describe a conditional part of a different use case that is only executed under certain circumstances

cards.newWord:
What can an activity diagram be used for in use case modeling?
A. To depict the sequence of activities executed by all the use cases in the system
B. To depict the sequence of external activities that the use case interacts with
C. To depict the sequence of active objects in a use case
D. To depict the activities in the main and alternative sequences of a use case
cards.definition: D. To depict the activities in the main and alternative sequences of a use case

cards.newWord:
How can a nonfunctional requirement be described in a use case model?
A. In a separate section of the use case description
B. As a use case precondition
C. As a use case postcondition
D. In a separate document
cards.definition: A. In a separate section of the use case description

cards.newWord:
What is a use case package?
A. A package describing the actors in the system
B. A package describing the use cases in the system
C. A group of related use cases
D. The package of objects that participate in the use case
cards.definition: C. A group of related use cases

cards.newWord:
What is an association?
A. A relationship between two classes
B. A relationship between two objects
C. A link between two classes
D. A link between two objects
cards.definition: A. A relationship between two classes

cards.newWord:
What is an association class?
A. a class models attributes of an association relationship
B. A class models an many-to-many association relationship
C. a class models an association between two or more classes. Its attributes are the attributes of the association
D. a association that models an association between two or more classes. Its attributes are the attributes of the association.
cards.definition: C. a class models an association between two or more classes. Its attributes are the attributes of the association

cards.newWord:
What is meant by the multiplicity of an association?
A. The number of associations in a class
B. The number of associations between two classes
C. How many instances of one class relate to how many instances of another class
D. How many instances of one class relate to a single instance of another class
cards.definition: D. How many instances of one class relate to a single instance of another class

cards.newWord:
What is an association class?
A. A class with multiple associations
B. A class with one association
C. A class that models an association between two or more classes
D. A class that models an association between two or more objects
cards.definition: C. A class that models an association between two or more classes

cards.newWord:
What is a generalization/specialization hierarchy?
A. A whole/part relationship
B. An inheritance relationship
C. An association between a generalized class and a specialized class
D. A layered hierarchy
cards.definition: B. An inheritance relationship

cards.newWord:
What is a composition hierarchy?
A. A weak form of a generalization/specialization hierarchy
B. A strong form of a generalization/specialization hierarchy
C. A weak form of a whole/part relationship
D. A strong form of a whole/part relationship
cards.definition: D. A strong form of a whole/part relationship

cards.newWord:
What is an aggregation hierarchy?
A. A weak form of a generalization/specialization hierarchy
B. A strong form of a generalization/specialization hierarchy
C. A weak form of a whole/part relationship
D. A strong form of a whole/part relationship
cards.definition: C. A weak form of a whole/part relationship

cards.newWord:
What is an aggregate message?
A. it represents messages sent at different times between the same pair of objects
B. it represents messages sent at different times between objects
C. it represents message. sent at different times of all objects
D. it represents messages sent at different times of an object
cards.definition: A. it represents messages sent at different times between the same pair of objects

cards.newWord:
What is an entity class?
A. A class on an entity/relationship diagram
B. A class that stores data
C. A class that interfaces to an external entity
D. An external clas
cards.definition: B. A class that stores data

cards.newWord:
What is a state-dependent control object?
A. An object that depends on a state machine
B. An object that communicates with a state machine
C. An object that controls a state machine
D. An object that executes a state machine
cards.definition: D. An object that executes a state machine

cards.newWord:
How would you determine a boundary class from the context diagram?
A. By looking at it
B. By selecting the external classes on the context diagram
C. By determining the software classes that communicate with the external classes
D. By drawing the boundary between the hardware and software classes
cards.definition: C. By determining the software classes that communicate with the external classes

cards.newWord:
What is a timer object?
A. An external clock
B. An internal clock
C. An object that is awakened by an external timer
D. An object that interacts with a clock
cards.definition: C. An object that is awakened by an external timer

cards.newWord:
What is the classification process for
application classes analogous to?
A. Categorizing books in a library
B. Deciding how many copies of a book are needed
C. Finding the classrooms in a school
D. Identifying what labs the school has
cards.definition: A. Categorizing books in a library

cards.newWord:
What is the purpose of a stereotype in class structuring?
A. To label a class according to its class structuring criterion
B. To identify the objects that belong to the same class
C. To distinguish between external objects and software objects
D. To identify the association between two classes
cards.definition: A. To label a class according to its class structuring criterion

cards.newWord:
What is a business logic object?
A. An object used in business applications
B. An object that defines business specific application logic
C. The internal logic of an object
D. A business object that determines whether a client request is logical
cards.definition: B. An object that defines business specific application logic

cards.newWord:
How is an actor depicted on an interaction diagram?
A. An actor has an association with the interaction diagram
B. An actor can provide input to or receive output from a boundary object.
C. An actor can provide input to or receive output from a boundary class.
D. An instance of an actor can provide input to or receive output from a boundary object.
cards.definition: D. An instance of an actor can provide input to or receive output from a boundary object.

cards.newWord:
What does a sequence diagram depict?
A. The sequence of external objects communicating with each other
B. Classes and their relationships
C. Software objects and the sequence of their interactions
D. The external objects communicating with the system
cards.definition: C. Software objects and the sequence of their interactions

cards.newWord:
What does a communication diagram depict?
A. The sequence of external objects communicating with each other
B. Classes and their relationships
C. Software objects and the sequence of their interactions
D. The external objects communicating with the system
cards.definition: C. Software objects and the sequence of their interactions

cards.newWord:
What is the instance form of an interaction diagram?
A. Depicts several object instances interacting with each other
B. Depicts one possible sequence of interactions among object instances
C. Depicts all possible interactions among object instances
D. Depicts all object instances and their links to each other
cards.definition: B. Depicts one possible sequence of interactions among object instances

cards.newWord:
What is the generic form of an interaction diagram?
A. Depicts several objects interacting with each other
B. Depicts one possible sequence of interactions among objects
C. Depicts all possible interactions among objects
D. Depicts all classes and their associations with each other
cards.definition: C. Depicts all possible interactions among objects

cards.newWord:
During dynamic interaction modeling, use cases are realized as follows:
A. Determine objects that participate in each use case and the sequence of interactions among them.
B. Determine external objects and the sequence in which they provide inputs to and receive outputs from each use case.
C. Determine sequence of interactions among use cases.
D. Determine how a use case is depicted through internal states and transitions between them.
cards.definition: A. Determine objects that participate in each use case and the sequence of interactions among them.

cards.newWord:
Which of the following interactions could happen on an interaction diagram?
A. An external user sends a message to a user interaction object.
B. An external user sends a message to an entity object.
C. An external user sends a message to an I/O object.
D. An external user sends a message to a printer object.
cards.definition: A. An external user sends a message to a user interaction object.

cards.newWord:
Which of the following interactions is NOT likely to happen on an interaction diagram?
A. A user interaction object sends a message to an entity object.
B. An input object sends a message to a state-dependent control object.
C. An input object sends a message to a printer object.
D. A user interaction object sends a message to a proxy object.
cards.definition: C. An input object sends a message to a printer object.

cards.newWord:
What kind of object would be the first object to receive an input from an external object?
A. A user interaction object
B. A proxy object
C. An entity object
D. A boundary object
cards.definition: D. A boundary object

cards.newWord:
What is a state in a state machine?
A. A recognizable situation that exists over an interval of time
B. A condition that is True or False
C. An input from the external environment
D. An output from the system
cards.definition: A. A recognizable situation that exists over an interval of time

cards.newWord:
What is an action in a state machine?
A. An occurrence at a point in time
B. A cause of a state transition
C. An interval between two successive events
D. A computation that executes as a result of a state transition
cards.definition: D. A computation that executes as a result of a state transition

cards.newWord:
What is an entry action in a state machine?
A. An action that is performed when the state is entered
B. An action that is performed when the state is left
C. An action that starts executing when the state is entered and completes executing when the state is left
D. An action that executes as a result of a state transition
cards.definition: A. An action that is performed when the state is entered

cards.newWord:
What is an exit action in a state machine?
A. An action that is performed when the state is entered
B. An action that is performed when the state is left
C. An action that starts executing when the state is entered and completes executing when the state is left
D. An action that executes as a result of a state transition
cards.definition: B. An action that is performed when the state is left

cards.newWord:
What is a condition used for in a state machine?
A. A conditional action
B. A conditional state
C. A conditional state transition
D. A conditional event
cards.definition: C. A conditional state transition

cards.newWord:
What is a state transition into a composite state equivalent to?
A. A transition into only one of the substates
B. A transition into each of the substates
C. A transition into none of the substates
D. A transition into any one of the substates
cards.definition: A. A transition into only one of the substates

cards.newWord:
What is a state transition out of a composite state equivalent to?
A. A transition out of only one of the substates
B. A transition out of each of the substates
C. A transition out of none of the substates
D. A transition out of any one of the substates
cards.definition: B. A transition out of each of the substates

cards.newWord:
How does a composite state relate to a substate?
A. A composite state is decomposed into substates.
B. Composite states are composed into substates.
C. A composite state transitions to a substate.
D. A substate transitions to a composite state.
cards.definition: A. A composite state is decomposed into substates.

cards.newWord:
If two actions are shown on a given state transition, which of the following is true?
A. The two actions are dependent on each other.
B. The two actions are independent of each other.
C. One action provides an input to the other action.
D. The second action executes when the first action completes execution.
cards.definition: B. The two actions are independent of each other.

cards.newWord:
What does a state-dependent interaction involve?
A. A control object
B. A state-dependent entity object
C. A state-dependent control object
D. A state-dependent user interaction object
cards.definition: C. A state-dependent control object

cards.newWord:
Which kind of object executes a state machine
A. Any software object
B. An entity object
C. A state-dependent control object
D. A statechart
cards.definition: C. A state-dependent control object

cards.newWord:
An input message to a state-dependent control object corresponds to:
A. An event on the internal state machine
B. An action on the internal state machine
C. A condition on the internal state machine
D. A state on the internal state machine
cards.definition: A. An event on the internal state machine

cards.newWord:
An output message from a state dependent control object corresponds
to:
A. An event on the internal state machine
B. An action on the internal state machine
C. A condition on the internal state machine
D. A state on the internal state machine
cards.definition: B. An action on the internal state machine

cards.newWord:
An interaction diagram should be developed for:
A. Only the main sequence of the use case
B. The main sequence and every alternative sequence of the use case
C. The main sequence and a representative alternative sequence of the use case
D. The alternative sequences of the use case
cards.definition: B. The main sequence and every alternative sequence of the use case

cards.newWord:
Which of the following could happen on an interaction diagram?
A. A state-dependent control object sends a message to an entity object.
B. A state-dependent control object sends a message to a coordinator object.
C. A state-dependent control object sends a message to a printer object.
D. All of the above
cards.definition: D. All of the above

cards.newWord:
If the same state machine is used in more than one use case, how is this modeled on interaction diagrams?
A. Develop one state-dependent control object for each use case.
B. Develop one state-dependent control object containing states from each use case.
C. Develop a hierarchical state machine.
D. Develop a coordinator object
cards.definition: B. Develop one state-dependent control object containing states from each use case.

cards.newWord:
How would two state-dependent control objects communicate with each other?
A. By sending messages to each other
B. By transitioning to the same state
C. Through an entity object
D. Through a proxy object
cards.definition: A. By sending messages to each other

cards.newWord:
An object can send alternative messages a or b to a state-dependent control object. How is this handled in the state machine?
A. One state with a different transition out of it for each incoming message
B. One state for each of the alternative messages
C. A composite state to handle the alternative messages
D. A substate for each alternative message
cards.definition: A. One state with a different transition out of it for each incoming message

cards.newWord:
In a system in which a client object executes a state machine and communicates with a service, which of the following is true?
A. The client has a state-dependent control object but the service does not.
B. The service has a state-dependent control object but the client does not.
C. Both the client and the service have state-dependent control objects.
D. Neither the client nor the service has a state-dependent control object.
cards.definition: A. The client has a state-dependent control object but the service does not.

cards.newWord:
Which of the following statements is NOT true for a component?
A. A composite object composed of other objects
B. An operation
C. A simple object
D. Provides an interface
cards.definition: B. An operation

cards.newWord:
Which of the following statements is NOT true for a component?
A. A composite object composed of other objects
B. A component includes operations
C. A component is either a composite object composed of a simple object
D. A component provides an interface
cards.definition: C. A component is either a composite object composed of a simple object

cards.newWord:
Which of the following statements is not true for Stereotypes?
A. modeling element can be described with more than one stereotype
B. During analysis modeling, one stereotype was used to represent the role characteristic of a modeling element (class or object)
C. During design modeling, a different stereotype can be used to represent the architectural characteristics la modeling element
D. All of the statements are false
cards.definition: D. All of the statements are false

cards.newWord:
What is a structural view of a software architecture?
A. A view in terms of a module hierarchy
B. A view in terms of components and connectors
C. A view of the physical configuration in terms of nodes and interconnections
D. A view in terms of objects and messages
cards.definition: B. A view in terms of components and connectors

cards.newWord:
What is a dynamic view of a software architecture?
A. A view in terms of a module hierarchy
B. A view in terms of components and connectors
C. A view of the physical configuration in terms of nodes and interconnections
D. A view in terms of objects and messages
cards.definition: D. A view in terms of objects and messages

cards.newWord:
What is a deployment view of a software architecture?
A. A static view in terms of a module hierarchy
B. A static view in terms of components and connectors
C. A view of the physical configuration in terms of nodes and interconnections
D. A dynamic interaction view in terms of objects and messages
cards.definition: C. A view of the physical configuration in terms of nodes and interconnections

cards.newWord:
What is a software architectural pattern?
A. The structure of the major subsystems of a system
B. The components and connectors in a software architecture
C. A small group of collaborating objects
D. A recurring architecture used in a variety of systems
cards.definition: A. The structure of the major subsystems of a system

cards.newWord:
What happens in a Layers of Abstraction pattern?
A. Each layer uses services in the layer immediately below it.
B. Each layer uses services in the layer immediately above it.
C. Each layer uses services in the layers immediately above it and below it.
D. Each layer is independent of the other layers.
cards.definition: A. Each layer uses services in the layer immediately below it.

cards.newWord:
What happens in a Call/Return pattern?
A. A calling operation in the calling object sends a message to an operation (a.k.a. method) in the called object.
B. A calling operation in the calling object invokes an operation (a.k.a. method) in the called object.
C. The calling object waits for a response from the called object.
D. The calling object does not wait for a response from the called object
cards.definition: B. A calling operation in the calling object invokes an operation (a.k.a. method) in the called object.

cards.newWord:
A producer sends a message to a consumer. Which one of the following is asynchronous message communication?
A. The producer waits for a response from the consumer.
B. The producer does not wait for a response from the consumer.
C. The producer goes to sleep.
D. The producer waits for a timeout.
cards.definition: B. The producer does not wait for a response from the consumer.

cards.newWord:
A producer sends a message to a consumer. Which one of the following is synchronous message communication with reply?
A. The producer waits for a response from the consumer.
B. The producer does not wait for a response from the consumer.
C. The producer goes to sleep.
D. The producer waits for a timeout.
cards.definition: A. The producer waits for a response from the consumer.

cards.newWord:
What is an integrated communication diagram?
A. A communication diagram formed by combining objects
B. A synthesis of all the communication diagrams developed to support the use cases
C. A communication diagram depicting the objects that realize a use case
D. A communication diagram that integrates the entity objects from the static model
cards.definition: B. A synthesis of all the communication diagrams developed to support the use cases

cards.newWord:
____ is an integrated communication diagram?
A. A communication diagram formed by combining objects
B. A communication diagram synthesis of all relevant use case based communication diagrams showing all objects and their interactions
C. A communication diagram synthesis of all the objects that realize a use case
D. A communication diagram that integrates the entity objects from the static model
cards.definition: B. A communication diagram synthesis of all relevant use case based communication diagrams showing all objects and their interactions

cards.newWord:
Which of the following objects should be assigned to the same subsystem?
A. Objects that are part of the same composite object
B. Client and server objects
C. User interface and entity objects
D. Objects that are associated with each other
cards.definition: A. Objects that are part of the same composite object

cards.newWord:
Objects that are in geographically different locations should be:
A. In the same subsystem
B. In different subsystems
C. In a composite subsystem
D. In layered subsystems
cards.definition: B. In different subsystems

cards.newWord:
If scope of control is used in subsystem structuring, then:
A. A user interface object is placed in the same subsystem as an entity object it updates.
B. A state-dependent control object is placed in the same subsystem as the objects it controls.
C. A state-dependent control object is placed in a different subsystem from the objects it controls.
D. A user interface object is placed in a different subsystem from an entity object it updates.
cards.definition: B. A state-dependent control object is placed in the same subsystem as the objects it controls.

cards.newWord:
How should an external object be designed to interface to the system?
A. It should interface to one subsystem.
B. It should interface to several subsystems.
C. It should interface to every subsystem.
D. It should interface to none of the subsystems.
cards.definition: A. It should interface to one subsystem.

cards.newWord:
A user interface subsystem is a type of:
A. Control subsystem
B. Service subsystem
C. Client subsystem
D. I/O subsystem
cards.definition: C. Client subsystem

cards.newWord:
Which of the following objects are NOT likely to be in the same subsystem?
A. User interface object and entity object
B. State-dependent control object and coordinator object
C. Business logic object and entity object
D. I/O object and state-dependent control object
cards.definition: B. State-dependent control object and coordinator object

cards.newWord:
Which of the following subsystems is NOT likely to be a client subsystem?
A. Control subsystem
B. User interaction subsystem
C. Service subsystem
D. I/O subsystem
cards.definition: C. Service subsystem

cards.newWord:
When is a coordinator subsystem required?
A. If the subsystem needs to coordinate several internal objects
B. If the subsystem needs to coordinate multiple I/O devices
C. If the subsystem receives messages from multiple client subsystems
D. If the subsystem needs to coordinate the execution of other subsystems
cards.definition: D. If the subsystem needs to coordinate the execution of other subsystems

cards.newWord:
When is a control subsystem required?
A. If the subsystem needs to control several internal objects
B. If the subsystem needs to control multiple I/O devices
C. If the subsystem needs to control multiple client subsystems
D. If the subsystem needs to control the execution of other subsystems
cards.definition: A. If the subsystem needs to control several internal objects

cards.newWord:
What is an information hiding object?
A. An active object that encapsulates data
B. A passive object that encapsulates data
C. A class that encapsulates data
D. A task that encapsulates data
cards.definition: B. A passive object that encapsulates data

cards.newWord:
What is a class interface?
A. Specifies the internals of the operations of a class
B. Specifies the externally visible operations of a class
C. Specifies the parameters of a class operation.
D. Specifies the signature of a class operation
cards.definition: B. Specifies the externally visible operations of a class

cards.newWord:
____ specifies the externally visible operations of a class, service, or composer internal structure of the operations
A. An interface
B. A class
C. An object
D. A service
cards.definition: A. An interface

cards.newWord:
Which of the following is NOT an object-oriented concept?
A. Information hiding
B. Class
C. Subclass
D. Subroutine
cards.definition: D. Subroutine

cards.newWord:
Which of the following is a class that realizes an interface?
A. The class calls the interface.
B. The class implements the interface.
C. The class is called by the interface.
D. The class is independent of the interface
cards.definition: C. The class is called by the interface.

cards.newWord:
Which of the following is an entity class?
A. An information hiding class
B. A subclass
C. A control class
D. A data abstraction class
cards.definition: D. A data abstraction class

cards.newWord:
What does a state machine class encapsulate?
A. A state transition table
B. A statechart
C. The current state of the machine
D. A state transition table and the current state of the machine
cards.definition: D. A state transition table and the current state of the machine

cards.newWord:
Which of the following is unlikely to be a graphical user interface class?
A. A menu
B. A window
C. A button
D. A pin
cards.definition: D. A pin

cards.newWord:
Which of the following is unlikely to be encapsulated in a business logic class?
A. A business rule
B. Calls to operations of an entity class
C. Deny cash withdrawal if balance of account is less than $10
D. A dialog box
cards.definition: D. A dialog box

cards.newWord:
Which of the following is NOT allowed through inheritance?
A. Subclass inherits attributes from superclass.
B. Subclass inherits operations from superclass.
C. Subclass redefines attributes inherited from superclass.
D. Subclass redefines operations inherited from superclass.
cards.definition: C. Subclass redefines attributes inherited from superclass.

cards.newWord:
Which of the following is true for an abstract class?
A. It is used as a template for creating objects.
B. It is used as a template for creating subclasses.
C. It is used as a template for creating classes.
D. It is used as a template for creating superclasses.
cards.definition: B. It is used as a template for creating subclasses.

cards.newWord:
In object-oriented design, polymorphism means that:
A. Different classes may have the same name.
B. Different classes may have the same interface name.
C. Different classes may have the same superclass name.
D. Different classes may have the same operation name
cards.definition: D. Different classes may have the same operation name

cards.newWord:
With polymorphism and dynamic binding, an object can:
A. Invoke operation of different names on the same objects.
B. Invoke operations of different names on different objects.
C. Invoke an operation of the same name on the same object.
D. Invoke an operation of the same name on different objects.
cards.definition: D. Invoke an operation of the same name on different objects.

cards.newWord:
What is a server?
A. A hardware/software system that serves customers
B. A subsystem that makes requests and waits for the responses
C. A subsystem that responds to requests from clients
D. A hardware/software system that provides one or more services for multiple clients
cards.definition: D. A hardware/software system that provides one or more services for multiple clients

cards.newWord:
The basic client/single service architectural pattern states that:
A. Multiple clients request services, and multiple services fulfill client requests.
B. Multiple clients request services, and a service fulfills client requests.
C. A client requests services, and a service fulfills client requests.
D. A client requests services, and multiple services fulfill client requests
cards.definition: B. Multiple clients request services, and a service fulfills client requests.

cards.newWord:
In a Multi-tier Client/Service architectural pattern, which of the following is
true about an intermediate tier?
A. An intermediate tier is a client tier.
B. An intermediate tier is a service tier.
C. An intermediate tier is both a control tier and a service tier.
D. An intermediate tier is both a client tier and a service tier.
cards.definition: D. An intermediate tier is both a client tier and a service tier.

cards.newWord:
How is Multiple Client/Multiple Service architectural pattern different from a
Multiple Client/Single Service architectural pattern?
A. A service can receive requests from multiple clients.
B. A client can send requests to multiple services.
C. A client can send requests to other clients.
D. A service can respond to requests from multiple clients.
cards.definition: B. A client can send requests to multiple services.

cards.newWord:
How is a sequential service designed?
A. One object that responds to requests from clients
B. Multiple objects that respond to requests from clients
C. One subsystem that responds to requests from clients
D. Multiple subsystems that respond to requests from clients
cards.definition: A. One object that responds to requests from clients

cards.newWord:
How is a concurrent service designed?
A. One object that responds to requests from clients
B. Multiple objects that respond to requests from clients
C. One subsystem that responds to requests from clients
D. Multiple subsystems that respond to requests from clients
cards.definition: B. Multiple objects that respond to requests from clients

cards.newWord:
What is a database wrapper class?
A. A class that encapsulates a data structure
B. A class that encapsulates a database
C. A class that encapsulates the details of how to access data in a database
D. A class that encapsulates a relational table
cards.definition: C. A class that encapsulates the details of how to access data in a database

cards.newWord:
When designing an entity class as a relational table, which of the following is
NOT true?
A. The relational table has multiple primary keys.
B. The relational table has multiple foreign keys.
C. The relational table has a primary key.
D. The relational table has a concatenated primary key.
cards.definition: A. The relational table has multiple primary keys.

cards.newWord:
When mapping an aggregation hierarchy to a relational table, which of the
following is NOT true?
A. The aggregate and part tables have different primary keys.
B. The aggregate and part tables have the same primary key
C. The primary key of the aggregate table is a foreign key of the part table.
D. The primary key of the part table is a foreign key of the aggregate table
cards.definition: D. The primary key of the part table is a foreign key of the aggregate table

cards.newWord:
When mapping a generalization/specialization relationship to a relational database, which of the following is NOT possible?
A. The superclass and each subclass are designed as relational tables.
B. Only subclasses are designed as relational tables.
C. The aggregate and part classes are designed as relational tables.
D. Only the superclass is designed as a relational table.
cards.definition: C. The aggregate and part classes are designed as relational tables.

cards.newWord:
What is a service-oriented architecture (SOA)?
A. A distributed software architecture consisting of multiple related services
B. A distributed software architecture consisting of multiple autonomous services
C. A distributed client/service architecture
D. A distributed software architecture
cards.definition: B. A distributed software architecture consisting of multiple autonomous services

cards.newWord:
Which of the following properties DOES NOT apply to a service?
A. Reusable
B. Discoverable
C. Fixed
D. Autonomous
cards.definition: C. Fixed

cards.newWord:
In a SOA, which of the following is NOT true?
A. A client communicates with a specific service provided on a fixed server configuration.
B. A client discovers and links to a service.
C. Multiple clients communicate with a service.
D. Standard protocols are provided to allow clients to communicate with services.
cards.definition: A. A client communicates with a specific service provided on a fixed server configuration.

cards.newWord:
What is an object broker?
A. An object that breaks into a system
B. An object that sends requests to other objects
C. An object that handles requests sent by other objects
D. An object that mediates interactions between clients and services
cards.definition: D. An object that mediates interactions between clients and services

cards.newWord:
Why does a service register with a broker?
A. So that service requesters can discover it
B. So that a service can interrogate the broker
C. So that the registry is up to date
D. So that the service can relocate
cards.definition: A. So that service requesters can discover it

cards.newWord:
When is it particularly useful to use the Broker Handle pattern in place of the Broker Forwarding pattern?
A. If the client only communicates with the service once
B. If the client needs to have a dialog with the service
C. If the client knows the type of service required but not the specific service
D. If the client needs to provide the broker with a handle
cards.definition: B. If the client needs to have a dialog with the service

cards.newWord:
Yellow pages brokering is useful when a service requester:
A. Needs to discover the location of the service
B. Knows the type of service required but not the specific service
C. Knows the specific service required but not the type of service
D. Needs to discover the broker
cards.definition: B. Knows the type of service required but not the specific service

cards.newWord:
What is a transaction?
A. Consists of two or more operations
B. Consists of one operation
C. Consists of two or more operations that are indivisible
D. Consists of two or more operations that are divisible
cards.definition: C. Consists of two or more operations that are indivisible

cards.newWord:
What is a compound transaction?
A. The compound transaction is indivisible.
B. The compound transaction is atomic.
C. The compound transaction is decomposed into atomic transactions.
D. The compound transaction is decomposed into subatomic transactions.
cards.definition: C. The compound transaction is decomposed into atomic transactions.

cards.newWord:
With a Negotiation pattern, which of the following is NOT true?
A. The client agent can propose a service.
B. The service agent can offer a service in response to a client agent proposal.
C. The client agent can request a service.
D. The service agent can offer a service in response to a client agent request.
cards.definition: D. The service agent can offer a service in response to a client agent request.

cards.newWord:
In a distributed component-based software architecture, which of the following statements is the most complete description of component deployment?
A. Component instances can be deployed to different nodes in a geographically distributed environment.
B. Component instances can be deployed to different nodes in a geographically distributed environment before design.
C. Component instances can be deployed to different nodes in a geographically distributed environment before implementation.
D. Component instances can be deployed to different nodes in a geographically distributed environment after design and implementation.
cards.definition: D. Component instances can be deployed to different nodes in a geographically distributed environment after design and implementation.

cards.newWord:
What does a component interface consist of?
A. The externally visible operations of a component
B. The operations provided by a component
C. The operations required by a component
D. The operations that a component supports
cards.definition: A. The externally visible operations of a component

cards.newWord:
What does a component's provided interface consist of?
A. The operations that a component must fulfill
B. The operations inside a component
C. The operations that a component uses
D. The operations of a component
cards.definition: A. The operations that a component must fulfill

cards.newWord:
What does a component's required interface consist of?
A. The operations that a component must fulfill
B. The operations inside a component
C. The operations that a component uses
D. The visible operations of a component
cards.definition: B. The operations inside a component

cards.newWord:
What does a connector join?
A. The provided port of one component to the required port of another component
B. The provided port of one component to the provided port of another component
C. The required port of one component to the provided port of another component
D. The required port of one component to the required port of another component
cards.definition: C. The required port of one component to the provided port of another component

cards.newWord:
What does a delegation connector join?
A. An outer provided port to an inner provided port
B. An outer provided port to an inner required port
C. An outer required port to an inner provided port
D. An outer provided port to an outer required port
cards.definition: A. An outer provided port to an inner provided port

cards.newWord:
What is broadcast message communication?
A. A message sent to several recipients
B. A message sent to a specific recipient
C. A message sent to all recipients
D. A message sent to recipients who are members of a group
cards.definition: C. A message sent to all recipients

cards.newWord:
What are the communication characteristics of subscription/notification?
A. A message sent to several recipients
B. A message sent to a specific recipient
C. A message sent to all recipients
D. A message sent to recipients who have joined a group
cards.definition: D. A message sent to recipients who have joined a group

cards.newWord:
During application deployment:
A. The application is executed.
B. Component instances are executed.
C. Component instances are assigned to hardware nodes.
D. Component instances are instantiated.
cards.definition: C. Component instances are assigned to hardware nodes.

cards.newWord:
What is an advantage of localized autonomy in component-based design?
A. If a component goes down, other components can continue to execute.
B. Components execute concurrently.
C. Components are distributed.
D. Components communicate using messages.
cards.definition: A. If a component goes down, other components can continue to execute.

cards.newWord: a passive object does not have a thread of control
cards.definition: D. An active object has a thread of control

cards.newWord:
What is an event-driven input task?
A. A task that executes every few seconds
B. A task that controls other tasks
C. A task that receives inputs from an external device when it generates interrupts
D. A task that checks whether there is new input from an external device
cards.definition: C. A task that receives inputs from an external device when it generates interrupts

cards.newWord:
What is a periodic task?
A. A task that responds to each message it receives
B. A task that is activated by a timer event
C. A task that is activated by an external event
D. A task that is activated by an input event
cards.definition: B. A task that is activated by a timer event

cards.newWord:
What is a demand-driven task?
A. A task that responds to each message it receives
B. A task that is activated by an internal message or event from another task
C. A task that is activated by an external event
D. A task that is activated by an input event
cards.definition: B. A task that is activated by an internal message or event from another task

cards.newWord:
What is a control task?
A. A task that control other tasks
B. A task that executes a statechart
C. A task that executes on demand
D. A task that controls I/O devices
cards.definition: B. A task that executes a statechart

cards.newWord:
What is a user interaction task?
A. A task that interacts with I/O devices
B. A task that interacts with users
C. A task that interacts with a user sequentially
D. A task that interacts with a user concurrently
cards.definition: C. A task that interacts with a user sequentially

cards.newWord:
Which of the following is true for a Centralized Control architectural pattern?
A. Control is divided among various control components.
B. It provides the overall control and sequencing of the system.
C. It provides overall control by coordinating several control components.
D. It provides overall control over various I/O objects.
cards.definition: B. It provides the overall control and sequencing of the system.

cards.newWord:
Which of the following is true for a Distributed Control architectural pattern?
A. Control is divided among various control components.
B. It responds to multiple requests from client subsystems.
C. It provides overall control by coordinating several control components.
D. It provides distributed control over various I/O objects.
cards.definition: A. Control is divided among various control components.

cards.newWord:
Which of the following is true for a Hierarchical Control architectural pattern?
A. Control is divided among various control components.
B. It provides overall control over several client subsystems.
C. It provides overall control by coordinating several control components.
D. It provides overall control over various I/O objects.
cards.definition: C. It provides overall control by coordinating several control components.

cards.newWord:
Which of the following is NOT a case of event synchronization?
A. External event
B. Internal event
C. Timer event
D. User event
cards.definition: D. User event

cards.newWord:
What is a software product line (SPL)?
A. A family of systems with some common components and some variable components
B. An assembly line
C. A family of identical systems
D. The software products marketed by a company
cards.definition: A. A family of systems with some common components and some variable components

cards.newWord:
What is an optional use case?
A. A use case with some optional steps
B. A use case that does not need to be developed
C. A use case that is required by some product line members but not others
D. A use case that can be chosen in place of a different use case in a SPL member
cards.definition: C. A use case that is required by some product line members but not others

cards.newWord:
What is a use case variation point?
A. A variable use case
B. A location in the use case at which change can occur
C. An alternative use case
D. A location in the use case where an alternative path can start
cards.definition: B. A location in the use case at which change can occur

cards.newWord:
What is a SPL feature?
A. A requirement or characteristic that is provided by one or more SPL members
B. A marketing need
C. A class provided by the SPL
D. A SPL use case
cards.definition: A. A requirement or characteristic that is provided by one or more SPL members

cards.newWord:
What is a SPL feature group?
A. A collection of features
B. A group of features with a particular constraint on their usage in a SPL member
C. A group of mutually exclusive features
D. A group of optional features with a particular constraint on their usage in a SPL member
cards.definition: B. A group of features with a particular constraint on their usage in a SPL member

cards.newWord:
What is a kernel class in a SPL?
A. An entity class in the SPL
B. A SPL class that stores essential data
C. A class that is required by all members of the SPL
D. An external class to the SPL
cards.definition: C. A class that is required by all members of the SPL

cards.newWord:
What two categories of stereotypes are used in modeling SPL classes?
A. Kernel and optional stereotypes
B. Optional and variant stereotypes
C. Common and variant stereotypes
D. Reuse and application role stereotypes
cards.definition: D. Reuse and application role stereotypes

cards.newWord:
How are feature conditions used in a SPL state machine?
A. A guard condition
B. A condition that is True or False
C. To identify if a feature is selected or not in the state machine
D. To allow state machine inheritance
cards.definition: C. To identify if a feature is selected or not in the state machine

cards.newWord:
What is a kernel system in a SPL?
A. A member of the SPL only composed of kernel classes
B. A member of the SPL composed of kernel classes and possibly some
default classes
C. A member of the SPL composed of kernel classes and possibly some optional classes
D. A member of the SPL composed of kernel classes and possibly some
entity classes
cards.definition:
B. A member of the SPL composed of kernel classes and possibly some
default classes

cards.newWord:
What does the SPL software architecture describe?
A. The software inside a family of buildings
B. The structure of a client/server product family
C. The overall structure of the software product line
D. The software product line classes and their relationships
cards.definition: C. The overall structure of the software product line

cards.newWord:
What do software quality attributes address?
A. Software functional requirements
B. Software nonfunctional requirements
C. Software performance requirements
D. Software availability requirements
cards.definition: B. Software nonfunctional requirements

cards.newWord:
What is maintainability?
A. The extent to which software is capable of being changed before deployment
B. The extent to which software is capable of being changed after deployment
C. The extent to which software is capable of being changed during development
D. The extent to which software is capable of being changed after development
cards.definition: B. The extent to which software is capable of being changed after deployment

cards.newWord:
What is modifiability?
A. The extent to which software is capable of being modified after deployment
B. The extent to which software is capable of being modified after initial development
C. The extent to which software is capable of being modified during and after initial development
D. The extent to which software is capable of being changed before deployment
cards.definition: C. The extent to which software is capable of being modified during and after initial development

cards.newWord:
What is testability?
A. The extent to which software is capable of being developed
B. The extent to which software is capable of being tested before deployment
C. The extent to which software is capable of being tested after deployment
D. The extent which the software is understood
cards.definition: B. The extent to which software is capable of being tested before deployment

cards.newWord:
Traceability is the extent to which a product:
A. Can be traced back to products of previous phases
B. Traced back to the requirements
C. Traced forward to implementation
D. Deployed to a hardware configuration
cards.definition: A. Can be traced back to products of previous phases

cards.newWord:
What is scalability?
A. The extent to which an application can grow
B. The extent to which the system is capable of growing after its initial deployment
C. The extent to which the system is capable of growing during development
D. The extent to which the system is capable of being scaled
cards.definition: B. The extent to which the system is capable of growing after its initial deployment

cards.newWord:
What is reusability?
A. The extent to which software implementation is reusable
B. The extent to which software is capable of being reused
C. The extent to which SPL technology can be introduced
D. The extent to which the software is common among a program family
cards.definition: B. The extent to which software is capable of being reused

cards.newWord:
Which of the following is not performance-related?
A. System response time
B. System throughput
C. System availability
D. System capacity
cards.definition: C. System availability

cards.newWord:
Which of the following is not addressed by a secure system?
A. System penetration
B. Denial of service
C. System scalability
D. System authorization
cards.definition: C. System scalability

cards.newWord:
Which of the following system problems does availability address?
A. Denial of service
B. Single point of failure
C. System throughput
D. System penetration
cards.definition: B. Single point of failure

cards.newWord:
In the service Discovery pattern, how do a client and service communicate with each other?
A. The client communicates directly with the service
B. The client communicates directly with the service by a serviceHandle sent from a Broker
C. The client communicates indirectly with the service through a Broker
D. The client communicates directly with the service without a Broker
cards.definition: A. The client communicates directly with the service

cards.newWord:
In the Broker Handle Pattern, how do a client and service communicate with each other?
A. The client communicates directly with the service
B. The client communicates directly with the service by a serviceHandle sent from a Broker
C. The client communicates indirectly with the service through a Broker
D. The client communicates directly with the service without a Broker
cards.definition: C. The client communicates indirectly with the service through a Broker

cards.newWord:
When only the superclass in the generalization/specialization relationship is mapped to the relational table. Which of the following is NOT true?
A. All the subclass attributes are brought up to the superclass table
B. The discriminator is added as an attribute of the superclass table
C. This approach can be used if the superclass has many attributes, each subclass has only a few attributes, and there are a small number (two or three) of subclasses
D. The superclass table includes only attributes of the superclass and attributes of subclasses
cards.definition: A. All the subclass attributes are brought up to the superclass table

cards.newWord:
When the superclass and all of the subclasses in generalization/specialization relationship are mapped to relational tables. Which of the following is true?
A. The same primary key is used in the superclass and the subclass tables.
B. Each table has a different primary key
C. The same primary key is used in the subclass tables.
D. The primary key of the superclass table is a foreign key in subclass tables
cards.definition: A. The same primary key is used in the superclass and the subclass tables.

cards.newWord:
In the Service Registration pattern, What information does the service need to register with the broker?
A. Service name
B. A description of the service
C. Location at which the service is provided
D. All of these answers
cards.definition: D. All of these answers

cards.newWord:
What is Middleware?
A. A layer of software that sits above the heterogeneous operating system provides a uniform platform above which distributed applications, such as client/server systems, can run
B. A class of software that sits above the heterogeneous operating system provides a uniform platform above which distributed applications, such as client/server systems, can run
C. A package of software that sits above the heterogeneous operating system provides a uniform platform above which distributed applications, such as client/server systems, can run
D. A component of software that sits above the heterogeneous operating system provides a uniform platform above which distributed applications, such as client/server systems, can run
cards.definition: A. A layer of software that sits above the heterogeneous operating system provides a uniform platform above which distributed applications, such as client/server systems, can run

cards.newWord:
What is a long-living transaction?
A. has a human in the loop
B. has a long loop
C. has a long time
D. has an infinite loop
cards.definition: A. has a human in the loop

cards.newWord:
What are mapped to relation tables?
A. Entity classes in a class diagram
B. Relationships in a class diagram
C. Attributes of an entity in a class diagram
D. State-dependent control classes
cards.definition: A. Entity classes in a class diagram
